title: Bin Packing (4 items, 2 bins)
summary: Distribute items across bins of fixed capacity, minimizing bins used.
problem_statement: I have 4 items with weights [4, 3, 2, 1]. I have 2 bins, each with capacity 5. Assign each item to a bin so no bin overflows. Use as few bins as possible — return whether 1 or 2 bins are actually needed and the assignment.
real_world_example: Loading boxes into shipping containers, allocating jobs to servers with fixed CPU/RAM budgets, or scheduling courses across classrooms with fixed seat counts.

expected_pattern.variables: x_{i,b} in {0, 1} — whether item i is in bin b; y_b in {0, 1} — whether bin b is used at all
expected_pattern.constraints: each item in exactly one bin (sum_b x_{i,b} = 1); per-bin capacity (sum_i w_i * x_{i,b} <= C * y_b)

expected_solution_summary: Total weight is 10, each bin holds 5, so both bins must be used. One valid assignment: bin 1 = {4, 1}, bin 2 = {3, 2}. Any partition that splits 10 into two ≤5 sums works.
learning_notes: Bin packing introduces *coupled* constraints: the per-bin capacity term ties the item-in-bin binaries to the bin-used binary y_b through the capacity right-hand-side. Get the coefficients wrong and the LLM produces a CQM that compiles but encodes a different problem — exactly the trap the validation harness exists to catch.
